figures.caption: Table 4: FACTOIDROUTE (FR) vs. SOFTCENTROID (SC) on all 13 nanoBEIR tasks. nDCG@10 (×100). FR routes strong-factoid queries to plain cosine and all others to SOFTCENTROID (K=3, α=0.524, τ=0.05). Rows below the mid-rule are the QA tasks where SC regresses most: FR recovers the majority of the loss. 20/26 cells positive vs. SC. Cost: 1.0× (no extra encoder calls).

Table 3: Small-sample generalization probe with the default SOFTCENTROID at K=3, α=0.5, τ=0.05 frozen across all 13 nanoBEIR retrieval tasks on both models. NanoBEIR is a 50-query random sub-sample of each BEIR task. Paired-bootstrap p-values use 10,000 resamples on per-query nDCG@10. At this sample size, per-cell p-values are noisy. Deltas that conflict in sign with the full-corpus evaluation, for example ArguAna on j-v5-nano at −2.16 here against +3.19 on the full 1,406-query corpus in Table 2, are non-significant at p=.07. Bold marks the single cell significant at p < .05. HotpotQA on j-v5-small at−3.24, p=.014 is the only statistically significant regression across both models. On j-v5-nano no cell crosses the 95% bar.

Figure 11: Program P22 (four-LoRA majority gate). Re-encodes the query under all four task-specialized adapters for retrieval, classification, clustering, and text-matching and admits the amp only if at least three LoRAs agree on top-1. A representative LORAFUSION-substrate program. It does not enter the universal frontier because hard-vote over heterogeneous LoRAs averages away the signal that any single LoRA carries.

Figure 14: Program P33 (similarity-floor first-sent amp). Adds a lower bound on cosine before the amp fires. Below the floor the program returns the cosine baseline. An early gating-axis exploration that established the gap-confidence pattern reused by later universal champions.

Figure 13: Program P32 (tail-gap weighted amp). Replaces the cosine weight in P1 with a similarity-tail gap w = sim(1) − sim(K), so amplification scales with the confidence margin between top-1 and the rest of the top-K. Member of the centroid family.

Table 9: Best per-(family, task) ∆ nDCG@10 (×100) on j-v5-small. Bold marks the all-family winner per column. § marks not applicable, since DOCCHUNK requires long structured documents and ArguAna and FiQA lack this.

Figure 22: Program P164 (query-projection rotation). Projects the query onto the top-K subspace via the percoefficient mean of ⟨q,di⟩ weights. A linear-algebraic alternative to the centroid update. Non-universal because over-rotation destabilizes SciFact ordering.

Figure 21: Program P103 (two-round first-sent amp). A multi-round member of the AMP family that re-retrieves with q′ before the second amp pass. Like P40, the second round drifts the query off-manifold. The search moved beyond this direction.

Figure 3: Cumulative best universal ∆nDCG@10 averaged over six cells as a function of generation. Of 259 registered programs, only those with positive mean improvement are plotted; the x-axis truncates at 6× encoder cost because no program beyond that cost improves over the baseline. The curve flattens after generation 30 and saturates after generation 78, suggesting the fixed-program frontier is exhausted. Section 4.4 subsequently opens a new axis by searching over routing policies rather than algebraic primitives.

Figure 19: Program P97 (top-2 chunked anchor). Uses both the top-1 and top-2 documents’ first sentences as anchors, max-pooling their similarities to the query before reranking. Captures the highest nano-SciFact delta of +0.51 nDCG@10×100 among 2×-cost programs but breaks ArguAna because the top-2 is often a counter-argument with high cosine.

Figure 20: Program P102 (gap-concentration amp). Replaces the gap-confidence gate with a top-K concentration measure. The amp fires when the top-K similarity mass is concentrated above a threshold. A robustness-witness variant that maintains the AMP body but tightens the admit set.

Table 13: Held-out full-BEIR cross-model validation, reporting ∆ nDCG@10 (×100) versus the cosine baseline. The SOFTCENTROID default uses K=3, α=0.5, τ=0.05. Fusion uses BM25 score-level mix at w=0.1, defined in Section 5.6. Significance: ‡ p < 10−3, † p < 10−2, ∗ p < 5 · 10−2 on a paired bootstrap with 10,000 resamples on per-query nDCG@10. All four models on SCIDOCS show stat-sig SOFTCENTROID default lift, with p ≤ 0.001 on three of four. On Touche2020 the fusion lever provides p < 10−3 compensatory lift on all four models. SCIDOCS is multi-relevance scientific IR where the centroid update directly captures relevance. Touche2020 is argument retrieval where dense baselines trail BM25 and the fusion lever provides compensatory lexical signal.

Figure 28: Program P203 (per-query lambda). Uses two LoRA adapters: the retrieval LoRA for the initial retrieval and centroid, and the text-matching LoRA for a per-query interaction signal. The sign of the Pearson correlation between the two LoRA similarity vectors determines whether the centroid update adds or subtracts. Oracle-best on j-v5-nano NFCorpus at +1.69 nDCG@10 ×100. Cost 2×.

Table 2: Headline frontier results, reporting nDCG@10 ×100. Top block: oracle-best per-cell frontier program discovered by the meta-program from 10,535 evaluated configurations on the four-task nanoBEIR slices used to gate the loop’s frontier admission. Middle block: default SOFTCENTROID at K=3, α=0.5, τ=0.05 applied with no per-task tuning, on full BEIR for the j-v5 family. Bottom block: cross-model full-BEIR validation on e5-base-v2. Significance: ‡ p < 0.001, † p < 0.01 on a paired bootstrap with 10,000 resamples on per-query nDCG@10. “Cost” counts encoder forward passes. § Nano-slice FiQA gains do not survive at full-BEIR scale on the j-v5 family. The full-corpus FiQA evidence is on e5-base-v2 in the bottom block at +0.47.

Figure 26: Program P77 (doc-chunk K=2 redundancy gate). Splits the top-1 document into four disjoint sentencewindow chunks and encodes each separately. The amp fires only if at least two chunks have cos(q, chunki) above the per-query median similarity over the top-100 pool. Representative of the DOCCHUNK substrate family. Cost ≥ 4×.

Table 15: SOFTCENTROID component ablation, reporting ∆ nDCG@10 (×100) over the cosine baseline at each one-parameter sweep around the default K=3, α=0.5, τ=0.05. Interpolation α and temperature τ carry most of the gain. Centroid size K is largely inert.

Table 17: Cross-model default-config validation on e5-base-v2 across all 13 nanoBEIR retrieval tasks at the 50-query BEIR sub-sample, reporting ∆ nDCG@10 (×100). Three SoftCentroid variants from the j-v5 frontier are applied unmodified. The K=10 variant is the strongest default on e5-base-v2, with a mean lift of +0.77 and 10 of 13 cells positive, reflecting that this embedding-model family produces a more diffuse localcluster geometry than the j-v5 retrieval LoRAs. Bold marks positive cells.

Figure 8: Parameter sensitivity of SOFTCENTROID on e5-base-v2 full-BEIR, showing ∆ nDCG@10 (×100) over the cosine baseline for each parameter value across four tasks (NFCorpus, ArguAna, SciFact, FiQA-2018). The paper default is indicated by a navy border. Green cells are positive lifts; red cells are regressions. (a) α sweep (K=3, τ=0.05): NFCorpus is stable for α ∈ [0.2, 0.5]; ArguAna scales monotonically. (b) K sweep (α=0.5, τ=0.05): smaller K is better on NFCorpus and ArguAna; large K introduces regressions on SciFact. (c) τ sweep (K=3, α=0.5): low τ gives the sharpest centroid and the largest ArguAna lift.

Figure 9: Program P1 (top-1 self-amplification). Encodes query and document under the retrieval LoRA, finds the top-1 document by cosine similarity, then updates the query as q′ = L2(q+ w d(1)) with w = cos(q,d(1)), and reranks. The minimal embedding-space PRF and founder of the AMP family. Cost 1×.

Figure 18: Program P76 (universal-min champion at 2×). Combines first-sentence anchor with a similarity-floor gate. The longest-defended 2×-cost universal-positive in the registry, with +0.29 nDCG@10 ×100 minimum across all six universal cells.

Figure 17: Program P47 (cross-LoRA top-3 overlap gate). Fires the amp only when the retrieval-LoRA and the text-matching-LoRA agree on the top-3 set. Established the cross-LoRA agreement pattern. Subsequent programs P49 and P110 extended this gate to triple-LoRA composition.

Table 18: Cross-model transfer on the 4-task nanoBEIR probe at the 50-query slice. The table reports ∆ nDCG@10 (×100) of SOFTCENTROID default and two FiQA-tuned variants on three monolithic embedding models. The full-BEIR validation in Table 5 uses e5-base-v2 as the strongest of these three. Bold marks positive cells.

Figure 25: Program P28 (norm-convergence amp). Iterates the top-1 amp and tracks the update norm ∥qi+1 − qi∥. Stops the moment the norm increases (convergence reversal), with a safety cap of six rounds. Oracle-best on j-v5-nano ArguAna at +7.18 nDCG@10 ×100. Cost ≤ 2×.

Table 5: Cross-model validation at full-BEIR scale on five monolithic embedding-model families across a tenfold parameter range. The table reports ∆ nDCG@10 (×100) of SOFTCENTROID default K=3, α=0.5, τ=0.05 and two FiQA-tuned variants. Significance: ∗ p < 0.05, † p < 0.01, ‡ p < 0.001 on a paired bootstrap with 10,000 resamples on per-query nDCG@10, reported on the four cells with stat-sig analysis. NFCorpus and ArguAna lift positively and statistically significantly on the e5 and gte families with the default at ≥ 100M. The algebra also extends to e5-small-v2 at 33M with no model-quality penalty, with ArguAna at +8.13, around seven times larger than the corresponding bge-small-en-v1.5 gain at the same 33M. Combined with the j-v5 results in Table 2, default SOFTCENTROID produces positive ∆ on NFCorpus and ArguAna across all seven embedding-model families tested at full-BEIR scale, namely j-v5-small, j-v5-nano, e5-small-v2, e5-base-v2, e5-large-v2, gte-base-en-v1.5, and bge-large-en-v1.5. bge-base-en-v1.5 is the only ≥ 100M case where the algebra is partial.

Figure 16: Program P40 (multi-round Rocchio). Iterates the centroid update for T > 1 rounds, re-retrieving after each query update. Ruled out: rounds beyond T = 1 drift the query off the document manifold and lose ranking accuracy. The search moved beyond the multi-round-PRF substrate early.

Figure 10: Program P19 (first-sentence anchor). Same skeleton as P1, but the top-1 document is truncated to its first sentence and re-encoded. The resulting first-sentence vector replaces the document anchor. The 2×-cost frontier holder on ArguAna among j-v5 programs.

Figure 2: Four representative programs discovered by the agentic loop. (a) P1: top-1 self-amp, founder of the AMP family, cost 1×. (b) P32: tail-gap weighted amp, SOFTCENTROID family, cost 1×. (c) P19: first-sent anchor, 2×-cost frontier holder on ArguAna. (d) P35: dropout ensemble, stochastic sampling baseline, cost M×. Programs (a)–(c) belong to the centroid super-family; (d) mean-pools unit-norm embeddings under dropout.
